narration_segments.text: It starts before the eyes open. The alarm has not sounded. The room is dark. The body lies in bed, and somewhere in the transition between sleep and waking — in that liminal space where dreams dissolve and reality assembles itself — the chest tightens. The stomach clenches. The jaw, which was loose in sleep, locks. The hands curl into loose fists beneath the pillow. There is no reason. There is no threat. The room is safe. The door is locked. The morning holds nothing unusual — the same commute, the same desk, the same meetings, the same drive home.

David teaches seventh-grade history. He is good at it. His students like him. His evaluations are strong. He has taught for fourteen years and he knows the material so thoroughly he could deliver most of his lessons from memory. And yet. Every morning at 6:47, when the alarm sounds, David's body launches into a state of activation that belongs on a battlefield, not in a suburban bedroom. His heart rate jumps from resting 62 to 94 within seconds of waking. His hands shake as he buttons his shirt. His stomach churns — he cannot eat breakfast, has not eaten breakfast on a school day in six years. On the drive to work, his grip on the steering wheel is so tight that his knuckles ache by the time he parks. David does not tell anyone about this. He does not tell his wife, who would worry. He does not tell his doctor, who would prescribe medication. He does not tell his friends, who would tell him to relax, to breathe, to stop overthinking, as though the electrical storm in his nervous system could be calmed by a bumper sticker. David's anxiety has a history.

The practice begins with orientation. Sit in a chair with both feet flat on the floor. Feel the contact between the soles of the feet and the ground. Press gently — not hard, just enough to activate the nerve endings in the feet that send information to the brain about gravity, stability, position in space. The feet on the floor are the first signal of safety: the body is supported. The body is grounded. The body is not falling. Now turn the head slowly to the right. Not stretching. Not exercising. Looking. Let the eyes scan the room — the wall, the window, the door, the objects on the shelf. Take time. Let the gaze land on specific things: the color of a book spine, the shape of a lamp, the texture of the curtain.

There is a moment in every nervous system's healing when the body surprises itself. It does not announce its arrival. It does not come during the practice — not during the counted breaths or the self-hold or the ventral vagal anchoring. It comes in the middle of ordinary life, in a moment so unremarkable that it almost passes unnoticed. For David, it comes on a Thursday. Third period. Twenty-seven seventh-graders in the room. He is teaching the Constitutional Convention — a lesson he has taught so many times he could deliver it in his sleep.
